title: Investigating pedestrian suggestions for external features on fully autonomous vehicles: A virtual reality experiment
authors: Shuchisnigdha Deb; Lesley J. Strawderman; Daniel W. Carruth
research_problem: Identify potential external features on fully autonomous vehicles (FAVs) that help pedestrians understand intended vehicle behavior at crosswalks, improve receptivity toward FAVs, and affect crossing behavior. The study addresses the communication gap between driverless vehicles and pedestrians who traditionally rely on informal communication methods like eye contact and hand gestures.
method_summary: Virtual reality experiment using a pedestrian simulator (Unity 3D, HTC Vive headset). Thirty participants walked across a virtual crosswalk in front of a FAV. A 4x4 randomized complete block design tested four visual features (walking silhouette, 'braking' text, flashing smile, no signal) and four audible features (horn, music, verbal message, no signal). Data collection included: baseline receptivity survey, post-experience receptivity survey, demographic questionnaire, pedestrian behavior questionnaire (PBQ), personal innovativeness scale, feature ratings, plus simulator-collected crossing time and waiting time.
method_family: VR experiment, randomized complete block design
tasks: Pedestrian crossing behavior at unsignalized crosswalk; Feature preference evaluation; Receptivity assessment
metrics: Feature ratings (7-point Likert scale); Crossing time (seconds); Waiting time before crossing (seconds); Pedestrian Behavior Questionnaire (PBQ) scores; Personal innovativeness scores; Receptivity questionnaire scores
claim_text: Pedestrian receptivity toward FAVs significantly increased with the inclusion of external features
limitations: Single controlled scenario with FAV approaching from one direction only; no other traffic present in virtual environment | Proposed features indicated safe crossing conditions only; vehicle stopped in every trial—did not test mixed signals or feature termination | Small sample size (n=30) limits generalizability | First-time exposure to FAV may not reflect long-term behavioral adaptation | VR environment may not fully replicate real-world complexity and stress
notes: VR validation confirmed pedestrians respond similarly in virtual traffic as in real traffic (Deb, Carruth, et al., 2017); Pedestrians walking speed in simulator (1.54 m/s) was faster than general walking speed (1.20 m/s), possibly due to uncertainty about driverless vehicle; Verbal message provided fastest crossing time while beep confused participants; Age 31+ found visual features necessary for FAV trust; younger participants (18-30) trusted silhouette and 'braking' signs most

title: Exploring factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersections in China
authors: Weihua Zhang; Kun Wang; Lei Wang; Zhongxiang Feng; Yingjie Du
research_problem: Understanding what factors affect pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at signalized intersections in China, to help propose countermeasures for reducing pedestrian-related traffic crashes
method_summary: A questionnaire survey was designed to collect pedestrians' socio-economic characteristics, trip-related features, and attitude variables in different crossing environments. Based on 631 valid samples collected from Hefei, China, a binomial logistic regression model was established using the Score test for variable selection to evaluate the impacts of contributing factors on pedestrians' red-light running behavior.
tasks: Analyze pedestrian red-light running behavior at intersections; Identify significant factors affecting red-light running decisions; Predict probability of red-light running behavior
datasets: SP survey data from Hefei, China (631 valid samples); Collected May-June 2015; Six typical signalized intersections near central business district or transit stations
metrics: Odds ratios; Statistical significance (p-values); Score test values
claim_text: Four variables significantly affect pedestrian red-light running behavior: trip purpose, time period in a day, attitude towards running red-light when in hurry, and attitude towards road facility quality affecting crossing behavior
limitations: Data collected in only one Chinese city (Hefei); results may not generalize to other cities or countries due to cultural and driving behavior differences | Sample size of 631 may be insufficient for some subgroup analyses | Only considered signalized intersections; other crossing facility types not examined | Study focused on individual crossing behaviors, ignoring impacts of pedestrian platoons | Self-reported survey data may contain recall bias or social desirability bias
notes: Published in Elsevier's Accident Analysis and Prevention journal; Supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China; Field survey conducted during peak periods on weekdays and weekends; Highest red-light running rates found in age group 19-25 years (84.9%), junior college education (82.8%), students (81.2%)

title: Roles of personal and environmental factors in the red light running propensity of pedestrian: Case study at the urban crosswalks
authors: Dianchen Zhu; N.N. Sze; Lu Bai
research_problem: This study examines factors affecting pedestrian red light running violations at signalized urban intersections by analyzing both personal factors (pedestrian demographics and behavior) and environmental factors (presence and behavior of other pedestrians, signal time, and traffic condition). The study addresses a gap in the literature where personal and environmental factors are rarely investigated together in the same model, and interaction effects between these factors remain unexplored.
method_summary: Video observation surveys were conducted at six signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong. Crossing behaviors of 6320 pedestrians during red signal phases across 652 cycles were captured. A random parameter logit model was developed to measure associations between red light running propensity and explanatory factors, accounting for unobserved heterogeneity in pedestrian behavior.
method_family: Random parameter logit model (binary logit regression with random parameters)
tasks: Analyze pedestrian red light running violation behavior at signalized intersections
datasets: Video observation data from six signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong - 6320 pedestrians observed during 652 signal cycles
metrics: Odds ratios, coefficient estimates, z-values, AIC (Akaike Information Criterion), statistical significance levels (1%, 5%, 10%)
claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity of red light running compared to males, with 64.3% probability that females have lower propensity than males
notes: The study is notable for combining individual-level (personal demographics, behavior) and cycle-level (traffic condition, signal time) factors in the same model. Key findings include the significant role of social influences - presence of companions reduces violations while presence of other violators increases them. The random parameter approach reveals substantial unobserved heterogeneity, particularly for gender effects. Hong Kong lacks pedestrian signal countdown displays, making this study valuable for understanding pedestrian behavior without such aids.

Key findings include the significant role of social influences - presence of companions reduces violations while presence of other violators increases them. The random parameter approach reveals substantial unobserved heterogeneity, particularly for gender effects. metrics: Odds ratios, coefficient estimates, z-values, AIC (Akaike Information Criterion), statistical significance levels (1%, 5%, 10%)
claim_text: Presence of other violators in the same cycle significantly increases red light running propensity (odds ratio = 4.023), indicating social influence and vicarious experience effects
